_protocol: Calibration topic — prospective. Populated 2026-04-11. Resolves on BEA advance GDP release for Q3 2026 (est. Oct 2026).

meta.title: US Recession by Q4 2026
meta.question: Will the US economy enter a recession (2 consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth) by Q4 2026?
meta.resolution: BEA official advance GDP prints for Q2 2026 (released ~July 2026) and Q3 2026 (released ~Oct 2026). Two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth = recession confirmed. NBER declaration is corroborating but NOT required for resolution — the quantitative criterion governs.
meta.horizon: ~6 months (Q3 2026 GDP advance print, est. Oct 2026)
_notes: Base rate: Post-WWII US recessions occur roughly every 6-8 years. We are ~3 years from COVID trough.
Polymarket consensus ~25% as of early April 2026.
Section 122 tariffs (10% universal + higher sector-specific) add demand uncertainty and supply-chain inflation simultaneously.
Fed at 3.50-3.75% has limited room to cut before reaching ZLB concern territory.
H1+H2 are 'recession yes' outcomes (H2 shallow, H3 deep). H4 is stagflation variant. H1 is no recession.
WARNING: H labeling is non-standard here. H1=NO recession. H2/H3/H4 are recession severity variants. Sum H2+H3+H4 = total recession probability.

tagConfig._docs: ECON tag = hard data releases (GDP, PMI, unemployment). DATA = any numerical feed update. POLICY = Fed/Treasury/fiscal action. RHETORIC = statements without action. EVENT = market events, institutional actions. ANALYSIS = derived/model output.

ECON._note: Neutral by default — direction depends on whether data is strong or weak. Let evidence text carry the signal.

POLICY._note: Stimulative policy (rate cuts, fiscal expansion) favors H1 over H2/H3. Stagflation (H4) is orthogonal to policy.

LABOR._note: Labor data is highly directional but depends on reading. Use indicator thresholds, not tag alone.

TRADE._note: Tariff escalation or trade disruption is net-negative. Favors recession outcomes over H1.

RHETORIC._note: Statements do not move posteriors. Flag as RHETORIC and hold.

_prior_rationale: Polymarket ~25% recession probability = useful anchor but subject to bias (prediction markets underweight tail risks in low-liquidity environments).
Historical base rate: ~15-20% chance of recession in any given 12-month window (post-WWII average). 6-month window is lower: ~8-12%.
Tariff shock adds ~5-8pp above base rate based on analogues (2018-19 tariff uncertainty, 1970s supply shocks).
PMI Manufacturing below 50 for several months is recessionary-adjacent but NOT recession — services PMI matters more for GDP.
Unemployment at 4.6% is elevated from cycle low but not yet at Sahm Rule trigger (typically ~0.5pp 3m-avg rise above prior 12m low).
GDP growth forecasts of 1.5-2.0% leave a thin margin. A 2pp downside shock from tariffs or consumer pullback would flip sign.
Split: ~55% no recession (H1), ~22% technical/shallow (H2), ~14% significant (H3), ~9% stagflation (H4).

This is a modest recession lean relative to Polymarket. Rationale: Polymarket ~25% bundles all recession types. Our H2+H3+H4 = ~45%, but ~20pp of that is in H2 which is shallow and hard to distinguish from 'stall speed' growth. Adjust downward for base-rate anchoring.

H1.label: No recession — GDP stays positive both Q2 and Q3 2026
H1.description: US avoids two consecutive negative GDP quarters. Growth may slow to near-zero (0.0-1.0% annualized) but does not go negative. Labor market softens but holds. Fed cuts 1-2 times as insurance. Tariff impact is absorbed via margin compression and partial pass-through. Resolves: Both Q2 and Q3 BEA advance prints are positive.

H2.label: Technical recession — 2 negative quarters, shallow (> -1.5% annualized each)
H2.description: GDP goes negative for 2 quarters but neither quarter exceeds -1.5% annualized contraction. Unemployment rises to 4.8-5.2%. Shallow enough that NBER may delay or decline to call. Consumer spending contracts modestly. Fed cuts aggressively (3+ cuts). Resolves: Q2 or Q3 GDP negative, neither quarter worse than -1.5% ann.

H3.label: Significant recession — unemployment crosses 5.5%, GDP -1.5% or worse
H3.description: Hard landing. GDP contracts -1.5% or worse in at least one quarter. Unemployment rises through 5.5% by Q4. Corporate layoffs accelerate. Fed pivots to emergency cuts. Credit spreads widen materially. Possible external shock amplifier (trade war escalation, financial contagion). Resolves: At least one GDP print worse than -1.5% ann AND unemployment >5.5%.

H4.label: Stagflation — negative GDP growth with CPI above 4%
H4.description: Worst case: tariff pass-through re-ignites inflation above 4% CPI while GDP contracts. Fed is caught between cutting to support growth and holding/hiking to contain inflation. Analogues: 1973-74, 1980. Requires tariff escalation beyond current 10% universal — sector-specific hikes (autos, energy) driving simultaneous supply contraction and price increases. Resolves: GDP negative for 2 quarters AND CPI year-over-year above 4%.

posteriorHistory.note: Initial priors. Weighted against Polymarket ~25% (all recession types). Our H2+H3+H4 = 45% reflects higher weight on downside given tariff uncertainty and thin GDP margin. Moderate confidence — wide uncertainty bands appropriate.
Evidence ev_011: Moody Analytics chief economist Mark Zandi says the Vicious Cycle Index, which h

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

posteriorHistory.note: Evidence ev_012: Q1 2026 GDP growth slowed sharply to 0.5% annualized (from 4.4% prior quarter);

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

labor_market.label: Labor Market Health
labor_market.description: Composite read on employment conditions. Key leading indicators: initial jobless claims trend, quits rate, hiring rate.

current.sahm_rule_indicator: unknown — needs current 3m avg vs prior 12m low

thresholds.sahm_rule_trigger: 3m avg unemployment >= prior 12m low + 0.5pp → recession signal
thresholds.claims_concern_level: 4-week average above 260k is elevated; above 300k is recessionary

manufacturing_pulse.label: Manufacturing & Industrial Activity
manufacturing_pulse.description: ISM Manufacturing PMI as primary signal. Below 50 = contraction. Below 45 = significant contraction. New Orders sub-index is leading.

current.context: ISM Manufacturing below 50 for multiple months as of April 2026

thresholds.new_orders_leading_signal: New Orders sub-index below 45 typically leads recession by 2-3 quarters

consumer_health.label: Consumer Demand & Confidence
consumer_health.description: Consumer spending is ~70% of US GDP. Confidence surveys are leading; retail sales and PCE are coincident.

current.context: Consumer confidence declining for 3 consecutive quarters as of April 2026

thresholds.confidence_recession_signal: University of Michigan index below 60, or Conference Board below 80, sustained 2+ months
thresholds.retail_sales_concern: 3-month rolling average negative in real terms

monetary_conditions.label: Monetary Policy & Credit Conditions
monetary_conditions.description: Fed funds rate, yield curve shape, credit spreads. Restrictive conditions precede recession with 12-18 month lag.

current.yield_curve_status: unknown — inversion history through 2024-2025 may already have front-loaded recession signal

thresholds.yield_curve_inversion_concern: 10y-2y below 0 sustained 6+ months → historically precedes recession by 12-24 months
thresholds.credit_spread_warning: HY spread above 500bps signals credit stress
thresholds.fed_pivot_signal: 2+ emergency cuts between meetings = recession acknowledgment by Fed

tariff_impact.label: Trade Policy & Tariff Shock Assessment
tariff_impact.description: Section 122 universal 10% tariff effective impact on GDP and inflation. Tracks escalation risk.

current.escalation_risk: MODERATE — retaliatory tariffs from EU/China could amplify

thresholds.escalation_to_h3: Universal tariff rises to 20%+ OR major trading partner imposes retaliatory tariffs on US services/agriculture
thresholds.stagflation_risk_trigger: Tariff-driven CPI above 3.5% while GDP slowing — puts Fed in impossible position

_design_rules: Each indicator is OBSERVABLE — a reported data point or official action, not a sentiment reading.
Thresholds are set prospectively before first update. Do not change thresholds after firing.
Governor flags unfalsifiable hypotheses as DEGRADED.
H1 = no recession. H2 = shallow recession. H3 = significant recession. H4 = stagflation.

tier1_critical.desc: BEA advance GDP print for Q2 2026 is negative (annualized real GDP < 0%). Observable: BEA press release, ~July 30 2026.
tier1_critical.note: First of two required quarters for technical recession definition. Alone, triggers H2/H3 watch but not resolution.
tier1_critical.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -20pp, H2 +10pp, H3 +7pp, H4 +3pp. If strongly positive (>1.5%): H1 +10pp, others proportional down.

tier1_critical.desc: BEA advance GDP print for Q3 2026 is negative (annualized real GDP < 0%), following a negative Q2. Observable: BEA press release, ~Oct 30 2026.
tier1_critical.threshold_fired: Q3 2026 advance GDP < 0% AND Q2 was also negative
tier1_critical.threshold_not_fired: Q3 2026 advance GDP >= 0%, OR Q2 was positive
tier1_critical.note: Confirmation of technical recession. Severity determines H2 vs H3 resolution. If both quarters worse than -1.5% ann, H3. If stagflation conditions co-present (CPI >4%), H4.
tier1_critical.posteriorEffect: If fired following negative Q2: H1 to near 0, H2/H3/H4 redistribute based on depth and CPI.

tier1_critical.desc: Sahm Rule indicator triggers: 3-month average unemployment rate rises 0.5pp or more above its prior 12-month low. Observable: BLS monthly unemployment release.
tier1_critical.note: Sahm Rule has triggered before or concurrent with every recession since 1970. Current unemployment 4.6% — need prior 12m low to calculate current indicator value. Approximate: if prior 12m low was 4.1%, current 3m avg of 4.6% would be right at threshold.
tier1_critical.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -15pp, H2 +8pp, H3 +5pp, H4 +2pp.

tier1_critical.desc: Fed cuts rates outside of scheduled FOMC meeting (emergency inter-meeting cut). Observable: Fed press release.
tier1_critical.threshold_fired: Fed announces rate cut not at a scheduled FOMC meeting
tier1_critical.threshold_not_fired: All rate actions occur at scheduled FOMC meetings
tier1_critical.note: Emergency cuts occurred March 2020 (COVID), Jan 2008, Sept 2007. Each preceded confirmed recession. Extremely high signal — Fed only acts inter-meeting when conditions deteriorate rapidly.
tier1_critical.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -25pp, H2 +10pp, H3 +10pp, H4 +5pp.

tier2_strong.desc: ISM Manufacturing PMI falls below 45.0 in any monthly reading. Observable: ISM press release, first business day of each month.
tier2_strong.note: PMI already below 50 (contraction). A break below 45 historically correlates with recession within 6 months. Below 42 is historically only seen during confirmed recessions.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -8pp, H2 +4pp, H3 +3pp, H4 +1pp.

tier2_strong.desc: ISM Services PMI falls below 50.0 for two consecutive months. Observable: ISM press release.
tier2_strong.threshold_fired: ISM Services PMI < 50 for 2 consecutive months
tier2_strong.threshold_not_fired: ISM Services PMI >= 50 or only 1 month below
tier2_strong.note: Services is 70%+ of US GDP. Manufacturing can contract without recession if services hold. Sustained services contraction below 50 is a strong recession leading indicator.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -10pp, H2 +5pp, H3 +3pp, H4 +2pp.

tier2_strong.desc: BLS monthly unemployment rate rises above 5.0% in official U-3 rate. Observable: BLS Employment Situation report.
tier2_strong.note: Current at 4.6%. A crossing of 5.0% would represent significant deterioration and likely partial Sahm Rule trigger. Strong signal for H3.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -10pp, H2 +3pp, H3 +5pp, H4 +2pp.

tier2_strong.desc: 10y-2y Treasury yield spread returns to negative (inversion) after having normalized, sustained for 4+ weeks. Observable: Fed H.15 release or Treasury Direct.
tier2_strong.note: Yield curve was inverted 2022-2024. Re-inversion after normalization would signal renewed credit stress or flight to safety. Less reliable as standalone indicator post-QE era.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -5pp, H2 +2pp, H3 +2pp, H4 +1pp.

tier2_strong.desc: Real retail sales (inflation-adjusted) are negative on a 3-month rolling average basis. Observable: Census Bureau Advance Retail Sales + CPI adjustment.
tier2_strong.note: Retail sales proxy for consumer spending. Negative real trend = consumers pulling back. Three months filters noise.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -7pp, H2 +4pp, H3 +2pp, H4 +1pp.

tier2_strong.desc: 4-week moving average of initial jobless claims exceeds 300,000. Observable: DOL weekly claims release.
tier2_strong.note: Claims have been historically low (~220k range). A sustained rise to 300k+ indicates significant layoff acceleration. Fast-moving signal — leads GDP by 1-2 quarters.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -8pp, H2 +4pp, H3 +3pp, H4 +1pp.

tier2_strong.desc: CPI year-over-year exceeds 4.0% while GDP growth is below 1.0% or negative. Observable: BLS CPI report + GDP print.
tier2_strong.note: Stagflation precondition. Tariff pass-through is the primary risk channel here. Fed is trapped: cutting accelerates inflation, holding deepens recession.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: If fired: H4 +15pp, H2 -5pp, H3 -3pp, H1 -7pp.

tier2_strong.desc: US raises universal tariff rate to 20%+ or announces major sector-specific tariffs (auto, energy, pharma) above 25%. Observable: Federal Register / USTR announcement.
tier2_strong.threshold_fired: Universal tariff >= 20% OR major sector tariff >= 25% on >$100B import category
tier2_strong.threshold_not_fired: Universal tariff remains at 10%, no major sector escalation
tier2_strong.note: Current universal rate is 10%. Escalation would compound GDP drag and inflation simultaneously. Immediate recession risk amplifier.
tier2_strong.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -10pp, H2 +3pp, H3 +4pp, H4 +3pp.

tier3_suggestive.desc: BLS monthly unemployment rate rises above 5.5% in official U-3 rate. Observable: BLS Employment Situation report.
tier3_suggestive.note: This is the H3 resolution threshold — 5.5% is specified in H3's definition. Crossing 5.5% discriminates H3 from H2 (H2 expects 4.8-5.2% range). If unemployment rises but stays below 5.5%, that's H2 territory.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H3 +12pp, H2 -8pp, H1 -3pp, H4 +1pp. Key discriminator between shallow (H2) and significant (H3) recession.

tier3_suggestive.desc: BEA advance GDP print for any quarter shows contraction worse than -1.5% annualized. Observable: BEA press release.
tier3_suggestive.note: H3 requires at least one quarter worse than -1.5%. H2 is explicitly defined as shallow (> -1.5% each quarter). This is the mechanical discriminator between H2 and H3.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H3 +10pp, H4 +3pp, H2 -10pp, H1 -3pp. Hard discriminator: H2 is definitionally excluded if any quarter is worse than -1.5%.

tier3_suggestive.desc: BEA advance GDP print shows negative growth but milder than -1.0% annualized (between -1.0% and 0%). Observable: BEA press release.
tier3_suggestive.note: A mildly negative quarter is H2 territory — technical recession but shallow. Distinguishes H2 from both H1 (positive GDP) and H3 (deep contraction). NBER may not even call this a recession.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H2 +10pp, H1 -5pp, H3 -4pp, H4 -1pp. Favors H2 specifically — negative enough to rule out H1, mild enough to rule out H3.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index falls below 80. Observable: Conference Board monthly release.
tier3_suggestive.note: Confidence has been declining 3 quarters. Below 80 is historically associated with recession conditions. Sentiment indicator — leads spending by 1-2 months.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -3pp, H2 +1pp, H3 +1pp, H4 +1pp.

tier3_suggestive.desc: University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment falls below 60. Observable: U of Michigan monthly preliminary release.
tier3_suggestive.note: Below 60 is historically recessionary territory. Fast-moving; can recover quickly. Corroborating signal, not standalone.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -2pp, H2 +1pp, H3 +0.5pp, H4 +0.5pp.

tier3_suggestive.desc: ISM Manufacturing New Orders sub-index falls below 45. Observable: ISM press release (sub-component).
tier3_suggestive.note: New Orders leads the headline PMI by 1-2 months. Below 45 signals demand destruction, not just inventory adjustment.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -3pp, H2 +1pp, H3 +1pp, H4 +1pp.

tier3_suggestive.desc: S&P 500 falls 20%+ from its most recent high (bear market territory). Observable: major financial data sources.
tier3_suggestive.note: Markets are imperfect recession predictors (Samuelson: 'predicted 9 of last 5 recessions'). But a genuine bear market reflects earnings expectations. Corroborating, not causal.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -4pp, H2 +2pp, H3 +1pp, H4 +1pp.

tier3_suggestive.desc: ICE BofA US High Yield Index option-adjusted spread exceeds 500 basis points. Observable: Fed FRED series (BAMLH0A0HYM2).
tier3_suggestive.note: HY spreads widened during COVID (>1000bps), GFC (>2000bps), 2015-16 oil shock (~800bps). Sustained above 500 implies credit market stress, tightening conditions for leveraged companies.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -3pp, H2 +1pp, H3 +1pp, H4 +1pp.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Atlanta Fed GDPNow or NY Fed Nowcast falls below 0% for the current quarter. Observable: Atlanta Fed GDPNow (updated every few days).
tier3_suggestive.note: Real-time signal. Nowcasts are volatile early in a quarter and converge as data arrives. A sustained sub-zero nowcast (3+ weeks) is more reliable than a brief dip.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired and sustained: H1 -5pp, H2 +2pp, H3 +2pp, H4 +1pp.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Fed cuts rates 2 or more times in 2026. Observable: FOMC meeting statements.
tier3_suggestive.threshold_fired: 2+ Fed rate cuts occur at FOMC meetings in calendar 2026
tier3_suggestive.note: Multiple cuts signal Fed sees material downside. Insurance cuts (1 cut) are benign. Cycle of cuts (2+) reflects deteriorating conditions. Note: cuts alone do not prevent recession.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 +2pp (Fed response effective), but could also accompany H2 +2pp (cuts too late). Net effect small and ambiguous — mostly informational.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Single-week initial claims spike above 350,000, or 3+ major employers (Fortune 500) announce layoffs of 10,000+ in same calendar month. Observable: DOL claims data, company press releases.
tier3_suggestive.threshold_fired: Weekly claims > 350k OR 3+ major employer mass layoffs in one month
tier3_suggestive.note: Layoff waves are visible in claims data but lag announcement. Fortune 500 announcements are leading — actual separations take months.
tier3_suggestive.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 -4pp, H2 +2pp, H3 +1pp, H4 +1pp.

anti_indicators.desc: Q1 2026 GDP advance print comes in above 2.0% annualized. Observable: BEA advance GDP release ~April 30 2026.
anti_indicators.note: Strong Q1 gives economy a larger buffer before recession is possible by Q4. Recession by Q4 would require Q2 AND Q3 to both go negative — more runway needed.
anti_indicators.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 +8pp, H2 -4pp, H3 -3pp, H4 -1pp.

anti_indicators.desc: Unemployment rate holds below 4.5% for 3 consecutive months despite slowdown. Observable: BLS monthly reports.
anti_indicators.threshold_not_fired: Unemployment rises above 4.5% or data insufficient
anti_indicators.note: Labor market stability is the primary mechanism by which recessions stay shallow or are avoided. Consumers keep spending if they have jobs. Strong anti-H3/H4 signal.
anti_indicators.posteriorEffect: If fired: H3 -8pp, H4 -4pp, H1 +7pp, H2 -3pp... wait — normalizes to: H1 +8pp, H2 -3pp, H3 -4pp, H4 -1pp.

anti_indicators.desc: CPI year-over-year falls below 2.5% for two consecutive months. Observable: BLS CPI report.
anti_indicators.threshold_not_fired: CPI YoY >= 2.5% or only 1 month below
anti_indicators.note: Deflating inflation removes the stagflation risk (H4). If inflation is falling, tariff pass-through was weaker than feared or demand destruction is dominating supply shock.
anti_indicators.posteriorEffect: If fired: H4 -8pp, H1 +3pp, H2 +3pp, H3 +2pp.

anti_indicators.desc: US reduces or exempts significant portion of Section 122 tariffs. Observable: USTR notice, Federal Register, or White House announcement.
anti_indicators.threshold_fired: Universal tariff reduced below 7% OR major sector exemptions covering >$500B in imports
anti_indicators.note: Tariff rollback would remove the primary exogenous shock driving our recession premium above base rate. Immediate positive impulse for growth expectations.
anti_indicators.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 +7pp, H2 -3pp, H3 -3pp, H4 -1pp.

anti_indicators.desc: Unemployment rate stays below 5.5% through Q3 2026 despite negative GDP. Observable: BLS monthly Employment Situation reports through September 2026.
anti_indicators.threshold_fired: U-3 unemployment remains < 5.5% through Q3 2026 while GDP is negative
anti_indicators.note: If we get a recession but unemployment stays contained, that's H2 (shallow) not H3 (significant). The labor market is the discriminator between a technical recession and a painful one. H3 definitionally requires unemployment >5.5%.
anti_indicators.posteriorEffect: If fired: H2 +8pp, H3 -10pp, H4 -2pp, H1 +4pp. Directly discriminates H2 from H3 — recession but not severe.

anti_indicators.desc: ISM Services PMI stays above 52 for 3 consecutive months while manufacturing is contracting. Observable: ISM monthly report.
anti_indicators.threshold_fired: ISM Services PMI > 52 for 3 consecutive months
anti_indicators.threshold_not_fired: Services PMI falls below 52 or data insufficient
anti_indicators.note: Services resilience is the most common reason manufacturing recessions do not become economy-wide recessions. 2015-16 and 2019 examples where manufacturing dipped but services held.
anti_indicators.posteriorEffect: If fired: H1 +5pp, H2 -3pp, H3 -1pp, H4 -1pp.

actorModel.description: Recession outcomes are driven by the interaction of monetary policy (Fed), fiscal/trade policy (Executive/Treasury), and private sector behavior (business investment + consumer spending). Unlike geopolitical topics, there is no single decision-maker — outcomes emerge from aggregate behavior within policy constraints.

fed_powell.name: Federal Reserve / Jerome Powell (Chair through Jan 2028)
fed_powell.role: Primary monetary policy actor. Controls federal funds rate, forward guidance, balance sheet. Decisions affect cost of capital, mortgage rates, credit availability, dollar strength.
fed_powell.decisionStyle: Data-dependent, institutionally cautious, committee-based (FOMC). Resistant to political pressure but sensitive to labor market deterioration (dual mandate). Prefers gradual moves over large shocks. Will act aggressively if financial stability threatened (see: 2008, 2020).
biases: Recency bias toward last inflation episode — slow to cut if CPI sticky
Symmetric mandate rhetoric but historically more responsive to unemployment spikes than inflation undershoots
Adverse to 'stop-start' — dislikes cutting then hiking, creates credibility problem
Underweights tariff-driven inflation as 'transitory' supply shock — may ease even with rising CPI
filters: Dual mandate: price stability AND maximum employment. Cannot purely optimize for growth.
Committee process: 12 FOMC voters, requires majority. Dissents are possible but rarely determinative.
Communication lead time: typically telegraphs moves 1-2 meetings in advance
overrides: Financial system instability (bank failures, credit freeze) triggers emergency action outside normal cadence
Unemployment spiking rapidly (>0.5pp in one month) could accelerate easing
Political pressure from executive branch historically does NOT change policy but could affect forward guidance framing
keyWatchpoints: PCE inflation relative to 2% target at each meeting
Unemployment rate trend and claims data
Press conference language: 'watching carefully' < 'closely monitoring' < 'prepared to act' < emergency statement

treasury_bessent.name: Treasury Department / Scott Bessent (Secretary)
treasury_bessent.role: Fiscal policy framing, debt management, currency policy. Does not set tariffs (USTR does) but influences overall economic narrative and coordinates with White House on stimulus vs austerity framing.
treasury_bessent.decisionStyle: Institutionally oriented former hedge fund manager. Market-aware, less prone to pure political signaling than some predecessors. Likely to argue for 'orderly' tariff implementation rather than shock-and-awe.
biases: Dollar strength preference — strong dollar constrains export competitiveness but reduces import inflation
Deficit concern balanced against growth mandate
Creditor-class framing: prioritizes financial market stability over labor market
filters: Congressional budget process constrains fiscal response speed
DOGE-era spending constraints limit stimulus headroom
overrides: Severe market dislocation (2008-style credit freeze) would unlock emergency fiscal authority
Election-cycle proximity increases probability of stimulus proposals if polls deteriorate
keyWatchpoints: Treasury issuance announcements — refunding decisions signal fiscal path
Any statements on dollar policy or currency intervention
Coordination signals with USTR on tariff timeline

ustr_greer.name: Office of US Trade Representative / Jamieson Greer
ustr_greer.role: Administers Section 122 tariffs. Key escalation/de-escalation decision maker for trade policy. Tariff path is the primary exogenous variable in our recession model.
ustr_greer.decisionStyle: Politically directed — USTR implements White House trade strategy rather than acting independently. Will reflect Trump administration priorities: domestic manufacturing revival, leverage in bilateral negotiations, political signaling to rust belt base.
biases: Tariffs-as-leverage framing: treats tariff rates as negotiating chips, not permanent policy
Nationalist bias: default toward protection, burden of proof on free trade
Overestimates foreign elasticity — assumes trading partners absorb more of the burden than they actually do
filters: Legal constraints: Section 122 authority is time-limited and subject to congressional override
WTO dispute mechanism: formal complaints take years but create political friction
Supply chain lobbying: domestic industries dependent on imports (auto, retail, pharma) will pressure for exemptions
overrides: If recession attributed to tariffs in public discourse, White House may order quick rollback for political cover
Bilateral deal with China or EU would likely include tariff reduction component
keyWatchpoints: Any USTR press releases on tariff modifications
Retaliatory tariff announcements from EU, China, Canada, Mexico

congress.name: US Congress (118th/119th)
congress.role: Fiscal policy (spending, tax), potential tariff override authority, emergency economic legislation.
congress.decisionStyle: Slow, consensus-dependent, bicameral. Fiscal response to recession takes 6-12 months minimum from recognition to implementation. Tax cuts or stimulus packages require supermajority-equivalent alignment.
biases: Electoral cycle: midterm 2026 proximity increases urgency of economic optics
Partisan gridlock on deficit spending makes large stimulus unlikely pre-recession
Tariff skepticism in both parties (different reasons), but institutional deference to executive on trade
filters: Reconciliation process limits what can pass on simple majority
Senate filibuster on standalone economic legislation
overrides: Crisis legislation (CARES Act-style) passed quickly when recession already confirmed and unemployment rising fast
Lame-duck sessions sometimes enable otherwise-blocked legislation
keyWatchpoints: Midterm 2026 polling — affects willingness to countenance stimulus

business_sector.name: Aggregate Business Sector (capex, hiring, investment)
business_sector.role: Private investment is the most volatile component of GDP (~15%). Hiring and capex decisions aggregate into the labor market and industrial output readings we track. No single decision-maker — treat as a probability distribution responding to signals.
business_sector.decisionStyle: Forward-looking, risk-averse under uncertainty. Capex freezes often precede recession by 1-2 quarters. Hiring is lagging — companies maintain headcount through mild slowdowns, then cut sharply when outlook deteriorates.
biases: Policy uncertainty amplification: even without actual tariff damage, uncertainty freezes investment
Herding: once layoffs begin in one sector, cross-sector contagion accelerates
CEO confidence surveys often self-fulfilling — pessimism causes the slowdown it predicts
filters: Long-term contracts and supply chain commitments create inertia — can't adjust instantly
Labor market tightness means some sectors (tech, defense) are still hiring even as goods sector contracts
overrides: Credit market seize-up (banks stop lending) immediately constrains investment regardless of fundamentals
Tariff exemption rumors can temporarily unfreeze capex pending policy clarity
keyWatchpoints: Business investment (non-residential fixed investment in GDP accounts)
CEO confidence surveys (Conference Board CEO Confidence)
Announced capex guidance from S&P 500 companies in earnings calls

methodology: ACTIONS OVER RHETORIC — Fed rate decisions, GDP prints, and tariff notices move posteriors. Powell speeches and Treasury statements do not unless they announce concrete actions.
TAG EVERYTHING — separate POLICY (actual action) from RHETORIC (statement) in evidence log.
DON'T FRONT-RUN — wait for BEA GDP print before resolving. Nowcasts and forecasts are DATA, not resolution events.
SOCIALIZATION DETECTION — Fed 'leak' to WSJ often precedes moves. Watch Nick Timiraos reporting as a pre-signal.
TARIFF TRACKING — Federal Register is the authoritative source. White House announcements are RHETORIC until FR publication.
LAG AWARENESS — monetary policy acts with 12-18 month lag. Current tight conditions may already be causing Q2-Q3 deterioration before tariffs add their shock.

gdp_growth.label: Real GDP Growth (annualized, BEA advance print)

ism_manufacturing.label: ISM Manufacturing PMI
ism_manufacturing.baseline_note: Below 50 = contraction. Has been below 50 for multiple months as of April 2026.
ism_manufacturing.source: Institute for Supply Management (first business day of each month)

ism_services.label: ISM Services PMI
ism_services.baseline_note: Services has held above 50 longer than manufacturing.
ism_services.source: Institute for Supply Management (3rd business day of each month)

cpi_yoy.label: CPI Year-over-Year
cpi_yoy.baseline_note: Approximate recent reading. Fed target is 2% PCE (not CPI, but correlated).
cpi_yoy.source: BLS CPI (monthly, ~2 weeks after month close)

yield_curve_10y2y.label: 10-Year minus 2-Year Treasury Yield Spread
yield_curve_10y2y.baseline_note: Was deeply inverted 2022-2024. Positive spread = normal curve. Negative = inverted.
yield_curve_10y2y.source: Federal Reserve H.15 / FRED (DFII10, DFII2, or T10Y2Y)

sahm_rule_indicator.label: Sahm Rule Real-Time Indicator
sahm_rule_indicator.baseline_note: Triggered at 0.50pp. = 3m avg unemployment minus prior 12m low. FRED series: SAHMREALTIME.
sahm_rule_indicator.source: FRED SAHMREALTIME (updated monthly after BLS release)

consumer_confidence.label: Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index
consumer_confidence.baseline_note: Has been declining for 3 consecutive quarters as of April 2026. Below 80 historically recessionary.
consumer_confidence.source: Conference Board (last Tuesday of each month)

polymarket_recession_prob.label: Polymarket Recession Probability (any definition, by Q4 2026)
polymarket_recession_prob.baseline_note: Historical base rate ~15%. Current Polymarket consensus ~25% as of early April 2026.

initial_claims_4wk.label: Initial Jobless Claims (4-Week Moving Average)
initial_claims_4wk.baseline_note: Recent 4-week average. Threshold of concern: 260k. Recessionary: 300k+.
initial_claims_4wk.source: DOL weekly claims (every Thursday for prior week)

gdpnow.label: Atlanta Fed GDPNow Current-Quarter Estimate
gdpnow.baseline_note: Real-time nowcast. Volatile early in quarter, converges as data arrives. Q1 2026 estimate needed.

watchpoints: Q1 2026 GDP advance print (~April 30): first hard data point on tariff impact. Critical for Bayesian update.
April employment situation (~May 1): will Sahm Rule indicator move? Unemployment above 4.7% is notable.
ISM Manufacturing April reading (~May 1): watch for break below 45 or New Orders deterioration.
ISM Services April reading (~May 5): services contraction below 50 would be significant indicator fire.
Fed FOMC meeting outcome and press conference language: any 'data dependent' language shift toward easing is a leading signal.
Any USTR/White House announcement on tariff modifications, exemptions, or new sector tariffs.
Retaliatory tariff announcements from EU, China, Canada, Mexico — escalation amplifies recession risk.
Conference Board Consumer Confidence (~April 29): watch for sub-80 print.
Initial claims trend: any sustained move above 260k-270k range warrants note.
GDPNow Q2 2026 tracker once Q2 begins (April 1): early read on whether tariff shock is materializing.
Corporate earnings guidance for Q2 2026: forward guidance cuts signal CEO-level demand pessimism.
searchQueries: US GDP Q1 2026 advance estimate BEA
Federal Reserve FOMC rate cut 2026 outlook
University of Michigan consumer sentiment April 2026
US high yield credit spreads April 2026
US recession prediction market Polymarket April 2026
layoffs mass layoffs April 2026 WARN Act
US trade deficit tariff retaliation April 2026
Powell Fed inflation unemployment April 2026 speech

evidenceLog.text: St. Louis Fed President Musalem (AEI speech): Baseline 2026 scenario has real GDP growing close to potential, unemployment holding around current level, core inflation gradually easing toward 2% later in year. But risks tilt unfavorable — toward weaker labor market AND greater inflation persistence simultaneously. Atlanta Fed GDPNow projects Q1 2026 growth at 2.0%. Consumer spending soft in Jan-Feb (winter storms), but tax refunds +$1,000/household and wealth effects support spending.
evidenceLog.source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (Musalem remarks at AEI), April 1 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Confirms existing priors: GDP positive but thin margin, risks two-sided. Musalem explicitly describes 1970s stagflation analogy but stops short of predicting it. GDPNow 2.0% is firmly in H1 territory for Q1.
evidenceLog.note: Primary source (FOMC participant). Key signal: 'risks tilt in unfavorable directions' on BOTH mandates. This is the dual-risk framing consistent with H4 tail but baseline is H1.

evidenceLog.text: Musalem: Tariffs explain about half of excess 12-month inflation above 2% (St. Louis Fed staff estimates). Effect of 2025 tariffs should fade over next couple quarters, BUT geopolitical developments (Iran war) cloud forecast — now sees more risk of persistent above-target inflation throughout 2026.
evidenceLog.source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (Musalem remarks at AEI), April 1 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Tariff inflation quantified at ~50% of excess — this is the most specific Fed attribution to date. Iran war extends inflation persistence risk, which is H4-directional. But Musalem says tariff effect 'should fade' — that's H1-directional. Offsetting signals, no net posterior movement.
evidenceLog.note: Same speech as ev_001. Split into separate entry because tariff attribution and Iran inflation risk are distinct claims.

evidenceLog.text: Musalem on labor market: 'low hire, low fire' state. Payroll growth narrowly concentrated in few sectors, at low end of breakeven rate needed to prevent unemployment from rising. Firms reporting greater reluctance to hire amid geopolitical uncertainty (S&P Global Flash PMI, March 24). 'An increase in layoffs could lead to a rapid increase in the unemployment rate.'
evidenceLog.source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (Musalem remarks at AEI), April 1 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. H2/H3-directional: labor market at breakeven is one shock away from deterioration. But Musalem explicitly says unemployment remains 'close to the natural rate.' Not yet at indicator threshold (t1_unemployment_spike_5_5 requires 5.5%). Monitoring.
evidenceLog.note: FOMC participant explicitly flagging nonlinear labor risk. 'Rapid increase' language is unusual for Fed speeches — suggests higher-than-typical concern.

evidenceLog.text: Musalem on monetary policy: Supported holding at 3.50-3.75%. Real policy rate in 'lower portion of neutral range.' Could support easing if labor market weakens (provided inflation expectations stable). Could support HIKING if inflation or expectations move persistently higher. Current setting 'will likely remain appropriate for some time.'
evidenceLog.source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (Musalem remarks at AEI), April 1 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Confirms Fed in wait-and-see mode. Explicit hike scenario mention is notable — increases H4 tail plausibility slightly but this is a conditional statement, not a signal of intent. No indicator fired.
evidenceLog.note: Hike mention matters: first FOMC participant to publicly articulate rate hike scenario in a prepared speech since the easing cycle began.

evidenceLog.text: S&P Global US Manufacturing PMI: 52.3 in March (up from 51.6 in February). Third consecutive month in expansion territory. But firms reporting largest increase in selling prices since August 2022 — energy cost pass-through to customers.
evidenceLog.source: S&P Global Flash PMI, March 24 2026; Trading Economics
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Mixed signal: manufacturing expanding (H1 lean) but price pass-through at multi-year highs (H4 lean). PMI >50 is not recessionary. But pricing pressure in manufacturing is a leading indicator of CPI goods inflation. Net: offsetting.
evidenceLog.note: ISM PMI roundup confirms expansion for third month. Manufacturing expansion contradicts recession base case but price pressure is consistent with stagflation tail.

evidenceLog.text: March 2026 CPI: headline +0.9% m/m, 3.3% annual (highest since April 2024). Energy +10.9% (Iran war). Core CPI +0.2% m/m, 2.6% annual — below forecast. Real earnings -0.6% m/m. Tariff visible in apparel (+1%), airlines (+2.7%). Food unchanged.
evidenceLog.source: BLS CPI release via CNBC, April 10 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Cross-referenced with Fed Rate topic ev_007. Headline 3.3% is elevated but energy-driven (Iran), not demand-driven. Core 2.6% below forecast is H1-directional (disinflation in stickiest components). Real earnings -0.6% is H2-directional (consumer purchasing power erosion). Net: ambiguous, no posterior movement.
evidenceLog.note: Key for recession assessment: headline vs core divergence. If energy-driven inflation fades (Iran ceasefire), headline reverts. If core continues declining, Fed has room to cut (H1). If real earnings decline persists, consumer spending weakens (H2).

evidenceLog.text: Goldman Sachs raised US recession probability to 30% under current conditions (up from prior estimate). Cited tariff uncertainty and Iran war oil shock as primary drivers.
evidenceLog.source: Goldman Sachs via The Crypto Fire / financial media, April 2026
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. ANALYSIS tag — Goldman forecast is informative but does not move posteriors. 30% is close to Polymarket ~25% anchor. Goldman is a market participant, not a primary data source. Noted for tracking consensus shift.
evidenceLog.note: Goldman recession probability is widely followed but has mixed track record. Tagged ANALYSIS per framework. Weight 0.5 reflects PROPOSED claim from non-primary source.

evidenceLog.text: UCLA Anderson Forecast (Spring 2026): Signals 'meaningful shift in economic momentum since late 2025.' December 2025 forecast anticipated economy 'muddling through' amid tariff shocks and labor market stagnation. Spring revision suggests reacceleration driven by fiscal stimulus and AI investment.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. ANALYSIS tag. H1-directional: academic forecast sees reacceleration, not contraction. But pre-dates Iran war (Feb 28 onset) — may be stale. Weight 0.5 for ANALYSIS + temporal gap.
evidenceLog.note: UCLA Anderson is respected macro forecaster but this was issued 4 days after Iran war began — unclear if incorporated. Flagged as potentially stale.

evidenceLog.text: Tax Foundation analysis: 2026 Trump tariffs amount to average tax increase of $700 per US household. Trade deficit not meaningfully altered. NPR/CNBC one-year assessment: tariffs have not produced promised economic boom.
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Cross-referenced with Section 122 topic ev_010. $700/household is quantified consumer burden — H2-directional (demand drag). But not yet translating to GDP contraction per GDPNow (2.0% Q1). Trade deficit unchanged undermines the policy rationale but is not recessionary per se.
evidenceLog.note: Multiple-source convergence. Tax Foundation is nonpartisan policy research. Weight 0.8 (DATA from credible non-primary source).

evidenceLog.text: IMF World Economic Outlook April 2026: global growth forecast cut to 3.1% in 2026 (down 0.2pp from January). Assumes Iran war 'relatively short-lived.' Global inflation raised to 4.4%. Severe scenario (oil/gas prices +100-200% sustained into 2027): global growth only 2.0% — IMF calls this 'a close call for a global recession' (defined as <2%, happened only 4 times since 1980). Notes downward revision partly offset by reduced US tariff rates vs last year. Pre-war, global economy was performing better than expected.
evidenceLog.source: CNN (citing IMF World Economic Outlook, April 2026)
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. ANALYSIS tag — institutional forecast. 0.2pp global growth cut is modest and assumes short war. US-specific impact not broken out (global ≠ US GDP). Severe scenario (2% global) is conditional on oil spike — not base case. No US recession indicator fires. IMF's 'better than expected pre-war' note is marginally H1-directional. Net: informative for monitoring, no posterior movement.
evidenceLog.note: CNN effectiveTrust 0.7462. IMF is authoritative institutional source but ANALYSIS tag per governor rules. Weight attenuated: 0.7462 × 0.5 (ANALYSIS) = 0.37. Cross-reference: IMF severe scenario aligns with H4 (stagflation) if oil spike materializes domestically.

evidenceLog.text: Moody Analytics chief economist Mark Zandi says the Vicious Cycle Index, which has called every recession since WWII, just signaled the US is already in one. Goldman Sachs raised recession probability to 30 percent, Moody model at 48.6 percent.
evidenceLog.note: Domain keywords: index, probability, recession. No pre-registered indicator match — review whether this warrants a new indicator or evidence log entry. Source trust: 50% (moderate).

predictionScoring._docs: Calibration infrastructure. Snapshots auto-created by engine on each posterior update. Record outcome when BEA confirms or denies two consecutive negative quarters. Compute Brier score at resolution.

resolutionCriteria.resolvedYes_H2: Q2 and Q3 2026 both negative, neither worse than -1.5% ann, CPI < 4%
resolutionCriteria.resolvedYes_H3: At least one quarter worse than -1.5% ann AND unemployment > 5.5%
resolutionCriteria.resolvedYes_H4: Two negative quarters AND CPI YoY > 4% during contraction period
resolutionCriteria.resolvedNo_H1: Q2 OR Q3 2026 GDP positive (preventing two-quarter streak)
resolutionCriteria.resolutionDate_note: Q3 2026 advance GDP expected ~Oct 29-30 2026. Q2 advance expected ~July 30 2026 (first negative print would fire t1_gdp_negative_q2).

upstream.reason: Tariff escalation (H3) directly increases recession probability via demand destruction and inflation. Deal (H4) with material rollback reduces tariff drag on consumer spending. Tariff policy is the primary demand-side shock vector.
